title: Drag-to-reorder list with finger-tracked lift and gap animation
design_brief: A Things 3 / Reminders-style reorderable list: long-press a row to lift it (rises with shadow + scale 1.04), drag to a new position, other rows animate aside to make a gap. Drop with a satisfying snap. Reference: iOS Reminders reorder, Things 3, Notion mobile list reorder.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: react-native-draggable-flatlist (heavy, opinionated)
Animating other rows via setState (re-renders the whole list)
Hard snap-back on drop (should spring)
No haptic on lift / drop
Lift activated by short press (interferes with normal tap)
mobile_reasoning: Each row owns a translateY shared value. On long-press of any row, that row enters drag mode — its z-index lifts, shadow turns on, scale springs to 1.04. As finger moves, we compute which slot the row 'belongs' to now (based on absolute y), and other rows' translateY shifts to open a gap. All positional math is in worklets — React only re-renders when the drop commits the new order.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: DragList container manages a positionsMap shared value (row id → current slot). DragRow renders with absolute position derived from positionsMap.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const ROW_H = 60; const LIFT_SCALE = 1.04; const LONG_PRESS_MS = 350;
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: drop shadow on lifted row. Android: elevation 12 during drag.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Row height fixed; container width fills parent.

animation_strategy: Lift: scale + shadow opacity via spring. Other rows shift via withSpring with stagger via individual shared values keyed by id.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.LongPress(350ms) activates → followed by Gesture.Pan. Composed via Gesture.Simultaneous so the pan starts from the long-press point.
interaction_details: Long-press 350ms: row lifts with medium haptic, scales to 1.04, shadow appears
Drag: other rows shift to make gap with spring physics
Drop: selection haptic, row springs down to its new slot, onReorder fires
Tap (under 350ms): nothing (long-press required to enter drag mode)
Cancel mid-drag (gesture interrupt): row springs back to original slot
accessibility_considerations: Each row has accessibilityRole='button' + accessibilityActions=[{name:'moveUp'},{name:'moveDown'}] so VoiceOver users can reorder via standard rotor actions without the long-press gesture.
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Finger-tracked lift with haptic confirmation feels like picking up a real card. 2) Other rows' gap animation is UI-thread spring physics — no jank. 3) Drop snap with spring physics is the iOS Reminders convention everyone recognizes.

title: Nested bottom sheet: detail sheet stacks above primary sheet
design_brief: A Cash App pattern where tapping a transaction in the main bottom sheet opens a detail sheet that stacks ON TOP. The primary sheet stays at its snap, the secondary slides up with a slightly higher max snap. Dismissing secondary returns control to primary without re-entering. Reference: Cash App activity → transaction, Robinhood positions → detail.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Replacing the sheet content (loses scroll position)
Pushing a new screen instead of stacking sheets
Both sheets visible but pointer events leaking to bottom one
Backdrop visible only over secondary (should dim primary too)
mobile_reasoning: Stack of sheets needs a SheetStackContext that maintains an array. Top sheet has pointerEvents:'auto', sheets beneath have pointerEvents:'none'. Backdrop opacity comes from the topmost sheet's progress. Dismissal of top sheet pops the array; primary sheet remains visible at its snap. The pattern scales to N sheets — useful for drill-down flows.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: SheetStackProvider context. useSheet hook to push/pop. Sheet component subscribes to its position in the stack.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Each sheet uses snap-point ratios as in rn-002.

animation_strategy: Each sheet has its own translateY. Backdrop opacity = sum of all sheet progresses, clamped to 0.6.
gesture_strategy: Each sheet has its own Pan gesture. Only the top one is active; others wait their turn.
interaction_details: Push: secondary sheet slides up to snap higher than primary
Only top sheet receives gestures (pointerEvents controlled)
Backdrop only rendered once (under deepest sheet), dims further as more sheets stack
Drag-down on top sheet dismisses it; primary returns to its original snap unchanged
Multiple levels supported (capped at maxNested for sanity)
accessibility_considerations: Top sheet has accessibilityViewIsModal=true; sheets beneath have importantForAccessibility='no-hide-descendants'. accessibilityElementsHidden cascades correctly so VoiceOver only sees top sheet content.
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Sheet stacking is a Cash App / Robinhood pattern that maps to how iOS sheets in iOS 15+ can stack natively — we get the same UX without depending on the iOS 15+ system sheet API. 2) Underlying sheets keep their scroll position because they never unmount. 3) Pointer event control is fundamental to mobile layering — no z-index trickery needed.

title: FlashList masonry grid with variable-height image cards
design_brief: A Pinterest-style masonry grid: two columns on phone, three on tablet. Cards have variable heights based on image aspect ratio. Scroll smoothly through 1000+ cards. Reference: Pinterest, Unsplash app, Mubi.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: FlatList with numColumns (forces uniform row heights — breaks masonry)
Custom masonry that re-measures all cards on each scroll
React-native-super-grid (no recycling)
Loading full-resolution images (use thumbnail variants)
mobile_reasoning: FlashList supports masonry via the masonry prop (v2+). Each item provides a height hint via overrideItemLayout — FlashList places them in the shortest column. Image decode at thumbnail size via expo-image's contentFit + low-res transformation URL prevents memory pressure on long scrolls.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: MasonryGrid wraps FlashList. Card component memoized.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Columns derived from useWindowDimensions width > 700 ? 3 : 2.

animation_strategy: None during scroll. Optional: fade-in per card from rn-003 pattern.
gesture_strategy: Tap card to open detail (use shared-element from rn-005).
interaction_details: Smooth scrolling at 60fps with thousands of cards
Card images load lazily; offscreen cards unload from memory
Tap card opens detail via shared-element transition
Two columns on phone, three on tablet — same code
accessibility_considerations: Each card has accessibilityRole='button' with accessibilityLabel from title. Image hidden from VoiceOver (title carries meaning).
performance_notes: MasonryFlashList places items in the shortest column — no re-measurement during scroll. overrideItemLayout provides exact size to avoid measurement passes. expo-image cache means re-mounted cells render instantly.
what_makes_this_native: 1) True masonry recycling — Pinterest's own iOS app uses essentially this pattern with UICollectionView. 2) Per-column placement happens at insertion time, not on scroll. 3) Image cache means scroll-back is free.

title: Phone number input with country picker and live formatting
design_brief: A WhatsApp/Telegram signup phone field: country flag + dial code picker on the left, formatted phone number on the right. Live formats as user types (e.g., '4155551234' → '(415) 555-1234' for US). Country picker opens as a bottom sheet with search. Reference: WhatsApp signup, Telegram, Signal.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: react-native-phone-input (legacy, not maintained)
Country picker as a fullscreen Modal (use bottom sheet)
Formatting on every keystroke without parsing
No flag rendering on Android (emoji flags don't render on most Android)
Forgetting +1 vs +44 vs +972 dial codes
mobile_reasoning: Use libphonenumber-js for parsing — it's the reference implementation. Format as you type via AsYouType. Country picker is a bottom sheet with a sectioned list. Flags on iOS render via emoji; on Android most devices don't ship flag emojis — fallback to SVG country icons or country code as text.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PhoneInput composes CountryButton + TextInput. CountryPicker is bottom sheet with FlashList.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: Flag rendering branches on Platform.OS.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Field fills width minus margins.

animation_strategy: Country pick: brief spring scale on flag.
gesture_strategy: None custom.
interaction_details: Selection haptic on country picker tap
Flag scales 1→1.2→1 spring when country changes
Live formatting per AsYouType
Submit triggers if parsed.isValid()
System keyboard shows phone-pad layout
accessibility_considerations: Country button has accessibilityRole='button' accessibilityLabel='Country code, currently ${name}'. Input has accessibilityLabel='Phone number' with accessibilityHint about format.
performance_notes: libphonenumber-js bundle ~150KB — acceptable for the value it provides. AsYouType reuses the parser instance. No re-render of country button on text change.
what_makes_this_native: 1) System phone-pad keyboard with tel autocomplete suggests SIM-stored numbers. 2) Live formatting feels like the phone is helping you. 3) Platform-aware flag rendering — iOS emoji, Android code fallback.

title: Drawer + tabs hybrid: side drawer for sections, bottom tabs for context
design_brief: A Slack-style navigation: a left drawer for switching between workspaces / sections, bottom tabs for the current section's primary screens (home, search, profile). Drawer slides over with backdrop dim; bottom tabs stay anchored. Reference: Slack, Discord, Microsoft Teams.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Drawer + tabs implemented as two separate libraries fighting each other
Drawer that resizes content instead of overlaying
Tabs that hide when drawer is open (jarring)
Drawer behind tab bar (z-order error)
mobile_reasoning: Drawer is a sibling-overlay component with its own translateX shared value, driven by edge-pan from left or by a hamburger tap. Tabs from Expo Router live below. Drawer's z-index is higher than tabs but it appears 'above' content cleanly. Backdrop renders between drawer and content.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: AppShell wraps Slot (Expo Router tabs) + Drawer overlay.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: BlurView drawer surface. Android: solid dark surface.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Drawer width = min(300, SCREEN_WIDTH * 0.85).

animation_strategy: translateX shared value [-DRAWER_WIDTH, 0]. Backdrop opacity derived. Spring on snap.
gesture_strategy: Edge-pan Gesture.Pan with hitSlop on left edge. Gesture.Pan on opened drawer to drag-close.
interaction_details: Edge swipe from left activates drawer pull
Hamburger tap: selection haptic + drawer slides open
Tap scrim: drawer closes
Drag drawer past 50%: opens; below: closes
Backdrop dims to 0.5 max
accessibility_considerations: Drawer has accessibilityViewIsModal=true when open. Hamburger button has accessibilityLabel='Open navigation'. Scrim has accessibilityRole='button' label 'Close navigation'.
performance_notes: Single shared value drives drawer + scrim. Edge gesture only active for narrow hitSlop — no conflict with content gestures. Drawer mounts always but is offscreen.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Edge-pan-to-open is the iOS convention. 2) Drawer + tabs coexisting without conflict is Slack's actual pattern. 3) iOS native blur on drawer surface.

title: Skeleton loader that mirrors the actual layout, not generic blocks
design_brief: A feed loading state: each skeleton card matches the exact dimensions and arrangement of the real card it will become. The skeleton blocks shimmer with a left-to-right gradient sweep, and individual cards animate in with stagger before content loads. Reference: Facebook iOS, LinkedIn mobile, Apple Music search loading.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: A single skeleton block per card (lazy)
ActivityIndicator centered on a blank screen
Shimmer that pauses (should be continuous loop)
Skeletons with rounded corners when the real card has square corners (mismatch on swap)
mobile_reasoning: Skeleton must MATCH the layout exactly so the swap to real content doesn't shift. Shimmer is a Skia LinearGradient that sweeps across via a repeating animation. All cards share a single shared 'shimmerProgress' value driving the gradient position — one animation engine, multiple subscribers.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Skeleton primitives (SkelBlock, SkelCircle). FeedCardSkeleton mirrors FeedCard layout.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Same layout as real card.

animation_strategy: shimmerProgress = withRepeat(withTiming(1, {duration: 1400, easing: Easing.linear}), -1, false). All SkelBlocks share this.
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Continuous shimmer sweep left-to-right every 1.4s
All skeletons in sync (shared shimmer value)
Layout EXACTLY matches real card — no shift on swap
Cards stagger-fade-in via Reanimated entering layout
accessibility_considerations: Wrap skeleton in View with accessibilityRole='progressbar' accessibilityLabel='Loading content'. Skeleton elements themselves accessibilityElementsHidden.
performance_notes: Single shimmer shared value drives N skeletons. Skia rect with gradient is GPU-cheap. No re-renders during shimmer.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Layout-matching skeleton means zero CLS — feels like content materialized. 2) Shimmer runs on UI thread at 60fps with N skeletons sharing one engine. 3) Skia gradient is cheaper than a View with overflow-hidden + animated child.

title: Confetti burst on success using Skia particles
design_brief: On a successful action (payment sent, achievement unlocked, level complete), burst 60 colorful confetti pieces from a point on screen with physics: initial upward velocity, gravity, rotation, and fade. Pieces have varied shapes (rectangles, stars). Cleans up after 2s. Reference: Cash App money sent, Duolingo lesson complete, Strava PR.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: A library import for one-off celebration moments
Confetti via dozens of Animated.Views (jank on Android)
Confetti that lingers visually after animation done
Same color/size/rotation for every piece
mobile_reasoning: Skia is the right tool — N particles drawn in one Canvas, all positions computed in worklets. useFrameCallback updates particle positions each frame based on velocity + gravity. After 2 seconds, the component unmounts itself.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Confetti component with imperative trigger via ref.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const CONFETTI = {count: 60, gravity: 800, initialVelocity: {min: 400, max: 900}, durationMs: 2200};
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: None — pure Skia.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Spawn point given by caller; particles drift naturally outward.

animation_strategy: useFrameCallback updates particle positions: vx const, vy += gravity * dt, rotation += angular vel. Opacity fades over last 600ms.
gesture_strategy: Touch is optional; the drawing exposes a semantic image label and avoids gesture-only meaning.
interaction_details: Burst from caller-provided point
60 pieces, varied color/size/rotation
Gravity pulls down, rotation continues
Last 30% of lifetime fades opacity
Component unmounts after 2.2s — no leftover render
accessibility_considerations: Wrap in importantForAccessibility='no'. Confetti is decorative — the success message itself carries the meaning for VoiceOver. Reduce Motion: skip entirely (don't burst at all).
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) 60 particles at 60fps — would be jank-city with 60 Animated.Views. 2) Self-cleanup avoids stuck overlays. 3) Trigger-via-ref is the imperative pattern for celebrations.

title: Photo grid picker with multi-select and recent photos preview
design_brief: An attachment picker like Instagram/iMessage: full-screen grid of recent photos (3 columns), tap to select (up to 10), selected photos get a numbered overlay (1, 2, 3...) showing send order. Reference: Instagram story picker, iMessage photo picker, Apple Mail attach.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using expo-image-picker UI directly (no multi-select customization)
Loading full-resolution images in the grid (massive memory)
Selected state without ordering (users can't tell selection order)
Slow scroll because Image components aren't recycled
mobile_reasoning: Use expo-media-library to enumerate the device's photos with low-resolution thumbnails. Render in FlashList grid with strict numColumns. Selection state in a useSyncExternalStore so individual cells subscribe to their own selection number — toggling one doesn't re-render the grid.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PhotoPicker uses FlashList + PhotoCell. Selection store separate.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: limited library access supported via permissions.accessPrivileges. Android: full access default but require READ_MEDIA_IMAGES on Android 13+.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: 3 columns on phone, 5 on tablet.

animation_strategy: Selection number badge: spring scale 0→1 on select.
gesture_strategy: Tap to toggle. Long-press to preview full-size in temporary overlay.
interaction_details: Selection haptic on toggle
Number badge springs in on select, springs out on deselect
Selected cells get blue overlay + thick border
Done button shows running count
Deselect removes from order; remaining selections renumber
accessibility_considerations: Each cell has accessibilityRole='button' accessibilityState={{selected: idx >= 0}} accessibilityLabel='Photo, ${date}, ${idx >= 0 ? `selected as item ${idx+1}` : 'not selected'}'.
performance_notes: FlashList recycles cells. expo-image with memory cache (not disk — photos are already on disk via MediaLibrary). Selection store only re-renders affected cells.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Direct device media access via expo-media-library. 2) Per-cell selection subscription scales to thousands of photos without re-renders. 3) Numbered selection order is the Instagram pattern users know.

title: Context menu: iOS 3D-Touch-style preview vs Android Material long-press
design_brief: Long-press on a list item to reveal options. iOS: scaled preview of the item + popover menu below (matching Apple's contextual menus). Android: bottom anchored menu (Material design). Reference: iOS Mail long-press, iOS Photos long-press, Android Gmail.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Identical menus on both platforms
Using Alert.alert as menu
Modal that doesn't scale-preview the source item (iOS)
Heavy blur backdrop on Android
mobile_reasoning: iOS users expect to see the source item lifted/scaled with a popover. Android users expect a bottom menu. Both triggered by long-press. iOS uses Reanimated for the preview lift + popover spring. Android branches to a Material bottom sheet menu.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: useContextMenu hook. ContextMenuHost renders the preview/menu overlay.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: This task IS the difference.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Menu width capped at 240 on iOS popover; Android always full-width.

animation_strategy: iOS: preview lift via spring(1→1.05). Popover fades + slides up. Android: bottom sheet via spring up.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.LongPress(450ms) on target.
interaction_details: Heavy haptic on long-press activation
iOS: source item scales to 1.05 + blur backdrop + popover slides in
Android: blackened scrim + bottom sheet slides up
Tap outside: closes
Tap option: selection haptic + closes
accessibility_considerations: Source items expose accessibilityActions matching menu options so VoiceOver users don't need the long-press. Menu has accessibilityViewIsModal.
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) iOS preview lift + popover is THE 3D Touch/long-press idiom. 2) Android Material bottom sheet honors the platform's menu conventions. 3) Hook-based API shared, platform-specific surface.

title: Focus order management for a multi-section screen
design_brief: A complex profile screen with header (avatar, name, edit), stats bar (followers, following), tabs (posts, saved, liked), then content. VoiceOver/TalkBack users should traverse in this logical order even if visual layout uses absolute positioning. Reference: Twitter profile, Instagram profile.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Trusting DOM order on absolute-positioned elements
Letting decorative icons get focus
No grouping (every element a separate stop)
Focus traps on tabs
mobile_reasoning: React Native provides accessibilityElementsHidden + importantForAccessibility (Android) + accessible={true} grouping. Manually setting accessibilityViewIsModal for sheets. Tab navigation via accessibilityRole='tablist'/'tab' so VoiceOver groups them and announces 'tab N of M'.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: ProfileScreen with explicit grouping containers and accessibility roles.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: Component-level sizing, spacing, and hit-target constants are named instead of hidden in inline styles.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: importantForAccessibility on Android maps to accessibilityElementsHidden on iOS (RN handles both via accessibilityElementsHidden).
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Layout-agnostic — a11y order independent of visual.

animation_strategy: Motion is bounded, cancellable, and tied to state changes the user can understand.
gesture_strategy: None custom.
interaction_details: VoiceOver order: header (grouped) → stats summary → bio → tabs → content
Tab switch announces the new tab name
Stats use 'summary' grouping so 'X followers, Y following' is one stop, not two
accessibility_considerations: This task IS the accessibility consideration. Key roles used: header, summary, tablist, tab, tabpanel. Grouping via accessible={true} on parents. announceForAccessibility on tab change.
performance_notes: Accessibility props are static — zero runtime cost beyond what RN does anyway.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Native VoiceOver/TalkBack roles for tablist/tab/tabpanel — screen readers handle them automatically. 2) Logical grouping reduces stops from 8+ to ~5. 3) announceForAccessibility on tab change keeps screen reader users informed.

title: Image preloading and viewport-aware decode for smooth feed scrolling
design_brief: A media-heavy feed that pre-decodes the next 5 images before they're visible to avoid mid-scroll decode jank. Reference: Instagram Reels prefetch, TikTok preloading, Pinterest pin loading.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No prefetch — images decode on first viewport entry
Prefetching ALL images on mount
Decoding at full resolution
Using a JS-thread queue (creates re-render storms)
mobile_reasoning: expo-image exposes Image.prefetch(uri). We hook into FlashList's onViewableItemsChanged with a buffer: prefetch the items at indices [maxVisible+1 .. maxVisible+5]. Prefetch result is cached on disk. By the time the user scrolls into view, the image is ready.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: useFeedPrefetch hook driven by viewable items.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Ahead count constant.

animation_strategy: Motion is bounded, cancellable, and tied to state changes the user can understand.
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Scroll: next 5 images decode in background
Reaching previously-viewed: instant render (memory cache hit)
No mid-scroll decode jank
accessibility_considerations: No effect on a11y; this is purely a performance optimization.
performance_notes: Image.prefetch is fire-and-forget on native; runs off the JS thread. prefetchedRef Set guards against duplicate calls. Memory-disk cache means images survive a scroll-away.
what_makes_this_native: 1) expo-image's native prefetch uses SDWebImage/Glide on iOS/Android — engineered for this. 2) Viewable items API is RN-specific. 3) Disk cache survives app restarts.

title: Editorial layout: magazine-style article reader with drop caps and pull quotes
design_brief: A long-form reading experience like NYT or The Atlantic mobile app. Serif body type, large drop-cap on first paragraph, pull quotes that span full width with a thick top border, image captions in small italic. Reference: NYT iOS, The Atlantic, Apple News+ articles.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: System sans-serif body for editorial content
Centered body text (left-aligned is more readable)
Tight line height (1.5+ is needed for serif at body size)
Pull quotes styled like regular blockquotes
mobile_reasoning: Loading a custom serif (Charter, Source Serif, or NYT Cheltenham) via expo-font is the difference between 'has serif' and 'has the RIGHT serif'. Body text 18pt with 28pt line height (1.55x). Drop cap is a single character rendered larger with negative line height to nestle into the body.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Article component composes Paragraph, DropCap, PullQuote, Figure subcomponents. Typography tokens.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const TYPE = {body: 18, bodyLine: 28, h1: 32, dropCap: 88, pullQuote: 22};
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: Font loading via expo-font on both. iOS may have system serifs; Android typically does not.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Body width capped at 600 on tablet, full on phone.

animation_strategy: Optional: fade-in on scroll for figures.
gesture_strategy: Vertical scroll only.
interaction_details: Vertical scroll, optimized for long reading
Drop cap nestles into first paragraph
Pull quotes break the reading rhythm with thick top border
Figures full-width with italic captions
accessibility_considerations: Body text scales with system font size (allowFontScaling default true). Drop cap has accessibilityLabel='${letter}' (otherwise VoiceOver might skip it). Figures with accessibilityLabel from caption.
performance_notes: Static layout — no animation cost. expo-font preloads serif at app start. ScrollView fine for long content (no recycling needed for read-once content).
what_makes_this_native: 1) Custom serif rendering via expo-font matches editorial app standards. 2) Reading width cap respects rfading research. 3) Drop cap as nested layout (not absolute positioning) reflows correctly at large text sizes.

title: Multi-step form with progress bar, step transitions, and validation per step
design_brief: A 4-step onboarding wizard. Step indicator at top fills as user progresses. Forward/back navigation animates horizontally with the next step sliding in. Each step validates before allowing forward. Back is always allowed. Reference: Stripe Connect onboarding, Robinhood account setup, Linear workspace creation.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Stack navigation push for each step (loses progress bar context)
No transition (jarring instant swap)
Single big form (no progressive disclosure)
Disabling back nav (users always need escape)
mobile_reasoning: All steps live in the same screen, rendered horizontally in a row that translates. Active step uses translateX to show/hide. Progress bar derived from step index. Each step is memoized; only the active one updates state actively.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Wizard component manages step state. StepProgressBar + step content.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Step width = SCREEN_WIDTH.

animation_strategy: translateX shared value = -currentStep * SW with withTiming(320ms, bezier curve).
gesture_strategy: Optional swipe-back via Gesture.Pan on screen edge.
interaction_details: Progress bar fills proportionally to step
Next: validate, slide forward, selection haptic
Invalid: error haptic, no transition
Back: slide back (always available except step 0)
Disabled CTA visually conveys 'fill the form first'
accessibility_considerations: Progress bar has accessibilityRole='progressbar' accessibilityValue={{min: 0, max: steps.length, now: current+1}}. Back button accessibilityLabel='Previous step'. CTA has accessibilityState={{disabled}}.
performance_notes: Single row of steps that translates — no re-render of inactive steps. Step content only re-renders when its own state changes.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Worklet-driven step transition stays smooth on Android. 2) Haptic differentiation between successful next vs validation error. 3) Slide animation gives spatial orientation that web wizards rarely do.

title: Two-finger rotate + pinch on photo with combined transform
design_brief: A photo editor zoom + rotate: two fingers can simultaneously rotate AND pinch-scale an image. Both gestures composed and run in parallel. Reference: Instagram story sticker rotate, Apple Photos editing.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Either rotate OR pinch, not both
Rotation that fights the pinch's scale
Gesture race instead of simultaneous
mobile_reasoning: Gesture.Simultaneous(Pinch, Rotation) lets both run together. Each writes its own shared value. Transform stack applies translate (center the focal point), scale, rotate, translate back. Settling on release with springs.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PhotoEditor component with image and gesture composition.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: Component-level sizing, spacing, and hit-target constants are named instead of hidden in inline styles.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Image fits screen with letterboxing.

animation_strategy: scale, rotation shared values. withSpring on release.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Simultaneous(Pinch, Rotation, Pan).
interaction_details: Two-finger pinch + rotate simultaneously
Two-finger pan (drag with two fingers) translates
On release: scale snaps to min 1, rotation settles, translate returns to 0
accessibility_considerations: Reduce Motion: skip springs, just clamp values. Reset button accessible via accessibilityActions=[{name: 'reset'}].
performance_notes: All transforms on UI thread via shared values. Gesture.Simultaneous composes without recognizer conflicts.
what_makes_this_native: 1) True multi-touch composition — pinch + rotate + pan working together. 2) Spring settling on release. 3) RN GestureHandler exposes native UIGestureRecognizer / Android equivalent.

title: Toast notification with swipe-to-dismiss and stacking
design_brief: In-app toasts (success, error, info) that slide in from top, auto-dismiss after 3s, and stack vertically if multiple appear. Swipe up to dismiss early. Reference: Sonner-style on web but truly mobile-native.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: React Native's deprecated ToastAndroid (Android-only, ugly)
Toasts as Modal (blocks UI)
No swipe-to-dismiss
Position above status bar (looks broken)
mobile_reasoning: Toasts are part of the screen tree above content. Each toast is its own component with entry/exit animations. The container manages a queue. Each toast has a Pan gesture for swipe-up dismiss.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: ToastProvider context. Toast component with type variants.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS shadow / Android elevation.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Toast width = SCREEN_WIDTH - 32, max 480.

animation_strategy: Entry: translateY from -100 with spring. Exit: translateY back with timing, opacity fade.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pan vertical for swipe-up dismiss.
interaction_details: Slides in from top with spring + fade
Type-appropriate haptic (success/error/warning)
Stack of multiple toasts (each offset by 68pt)
Swipe up past 30px or fast flick: dismisses immediately
Auto-dismiss at 3s
accessibility_considerations: Each toast has accessibilityRole='alert' so screen readers announce immediately. accessibilityLiveRegion='assertive' for errors, 'polite' otherwise.
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Haptic differentiation by type. 2) Real swipe-to-dismiss with velocity. 3) Native shadow/elevation per platform.

title: Infinite scroll with prefetch trigger and skeleton append
design_brief: A search results screen that fetches more results when the user scrolls within 800px of the bottom. While fetching, append 5 skeleton rows that swap to real rows as data arrives. No 'loading more...' text — skeletons are the signal. Reference: Twitter/X timeline, Reddit Apollo, Threads infinite scroll.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: onEndReached without onEndReachedThreshold (fires too late)
Loading spinner at bottom (looks dated)
Re-fetching when scrolling fast past trigger
Showing 'No more results' as a row if not actually paginated
mobile_reasoning: FlashList exposes onEndReached + onEndReachedThreshold. We track loading state in a ref to avoid duplicate fetches. Skeletons appended as virtual items in the data array — type='skeleton' rows render the SkelBlock from rn-036.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: InfiniteList wraps FlashList. Skeleton row reuses Skeleton primitives.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Skeleton count = ceil(visible height / row height).

animation_strategy: Skeleton shimmer continues until row is replaced (instant swap on load).
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Scroll within 50% of bottom: load next page
Skeleton rows appear at the bottom while fetching
Real rows replace skeletons in place (same array position)
No 'load more' button
End of data: no more skeletons, no error if done
accessibility_considerations: Skeleton rows are accessibilityElementsHidden. accessibilityLiveRegion='polite' on a hidden node announces 'loading more results'.
performance_notes: loadingRef prevents duplicate fetches during fast scroll. doneRef short-circuits when pagination is exhausted. FlashList recycles item and skeleton row types separately.
what_makes_this_native: 1) onEndReachedThreshold tuned to trigger before user sees bottom — feels instant. 2) Skeleton-based loading state matches the design system. 3) Recycling preserves performance through thousands of items.

title: Scroll-driven sticky header transformation
design_brief: A profile screen where as you scroll up, the large header (avatar + name + bio) shrinks: avatar shrinks from 80 to 32, name moves to the right of the avatar, bio fades out. The header becomes a compact navigation header. Reference: Twitter/X profile, Instagram profile.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Two separate header components (one expanded, one collapsed) cross-fading (jarring)
Animating via setState (jank)
Hard threshold transitions (should interpolate continuously)
mobile_reasoning: Single header component whose layout properties are all interpolated from scrollY. useAnimatedScrollHandler tracks scrollY on UI thread. All transformations (avatar size, name position, opacity) derived via interpolate.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: CollapsibleHeader component embedded in screen with ScrollView.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: respects safe area top inset for collapsed height.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Fixed.

animation_strategy: Single scrollY drives multiple interpolated styles via useDerivedValue chains.
gesture_strategy: None custom.
interaction_details: Scroll up: avatar shrinks and slides toward header left
Name slides next to avatar and shrinks
Bio fades out over first 50% of scroll range
All interpolations on UI thread — buttery smooth
accessibility_considerations: Header content always accessible; size changes don't affect VoiceOver order.
performance_notes: Single scrollY shared value drives multiple useAnimatedStyle hooks. All transforms on UI thread.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Single scroll handler, multiple derived values — the RN scroll-driven pattern. 2) Layout transformation feels physical, not boolean. 3) Safe area aware.

title: Custom progress bar with animated stripe pattern
design_brief: A download/upload progress bar with diagonal striped animation that flows continuously — communicates 'work in progress'. Stripes slow when paused. Reference: macOS install progress, Steam download UI applied to mobile.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: ActivityIndicator on top of progress bar
CSS-style background image (not available in RN)
Animating individual stripe views
mobile_reasoning: Skia draws repeating diagonal lines. The offset of the pattern is a shared value with withRepeat. Progress is a separate shared value drawn as a clipped rect over the striped fill.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: StripedProgressBar component.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Width fills parent.

animation_strategy: stripeOffset = withRepeat(withTiming(stripeWidth*2, {duration: 800, linear}), -1).
gesture_strategy: Touch is optional; the drawing exposes a semantic image label and avoids gesture-only meaning.
interaction_details: Continuous diagonal stripe flow at 800ms cycle when active
Slows to 4000ms cycle when paused
Progress fills from left
Stripes only within the filled portion (clipped)
accessibility_considerations: Wrap in View with accessibilityRole='progressbar' accessibilityValue={{min:0, max:1, now: progress}}.
performance_notes: Single Skia canvas. Stripe offset and clip path are derived values — UI thread. Path geometry recomputes on each frame but it's cheap arithmetic.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Skia GPU draws repeating pattern efficiently. 2) Worklet-driven offset stays at 60fps. 3) Indicates 'paused' state through animation speed, not just color.

title: Video player with scrubber, play/pause, and gesture controls
design_brief: A video player with custom controls: tap-to-pause/play, double-tap on left/right edges to skip back/forward 10s, pan horizontally to scrub, vertical pan on left = brightness, right = volume. Reference: VLC Mobile, MX Player, Instagram Reels scrubbing.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Default video controls (look generic)
Scrub via timeline tap only (no horizontal pan)
No gesture controls (users expect iOS-style brightness/volume)
Slow seek on every scrub pixel
mobile_reasoning: expo-av Video component with no native controls. Gesture overlay handles all interactions. Scrub throttled to actually update video at 200ms intervals (visual feedback continuous). Brightness/volume use expo-brightness and expo-av setVolumeAsync.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: VideoPlayer component with controls overlay.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: Brightness on iOS via expo-brightness; Android same API.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Fills screen; respects safe area for controls.

animation_strategy: Controls fade in/out on tap with timing 200ms.
gesture_strategy: Tap, double-tap, pan composed via Gesture.Race / Simultaneous.
interaction_details: Tap: toggle play/pause + show controls
Double-tap left/right half: skip ±10s with haptic
Pan horizontal: scrub video
Controls auto-hide after 3s
accessibility_considerations: Play button has accessibilityRole='button' accessibilityLabel based on state. accessibilityActions for skip back/forward.
performance_notes: Video playback is native. Gesture overlay is single View. Controls fade is worklet-driven.
what_makes_this_native: 1) System video decoding via expo-av — hardware accelerated. 2) Gesture-based controls match Reels/TikTok patterns. 3) Haptic skip feedback.

title: Custom map markers with animated halo and selection state
design_brief: A map with custom markers that pulse softly when 'live' (e.g., active rides on Uber/Lyft maps). Selected marker grows and the halo intensifies. Reference: Uber/Lyft driver markers, Find My friends.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Default red pin markers
Marker animations done via setState (won't run smoothly inside map)
Heavy custom marker views (slow on Android)
mobile_reasoning: react-native-maps markers can wrap Reanimated views, but be careful — too many animated markers tank performance. Best to use marker tracksViewChanges={false} after initial mount. Halo is rendered as a child View with continuous opacity/scale animation, frozen visually until selected.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: LiveMarker component with selectable state.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: Android marker view re-renders are expensive — tracksViewChanges=false critical.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Fixed sizes.

animation_strategy: Halo: useFrameCallback only when selected. Otherwise static.
gesture_strategy: Tap handled by Marker.onPress.
interaction_details: Selected marker has pulsing halo (grows + fades repeatedly)
Unselected markers are static
Tap to select
tracksViewChanges flips off after initial mount for performance
accessibility_considerations: Marker has accessibilityRole='button' accessibilityLabel describing what it represents.
performance_notes: tracksViewChanges=false stops the map from polling the marker view for changes (massive Android perf win). Halo animation only runs when selected (most markers don't animate).
what_makes_this_native: 1) tracksViewChanges optimization is a react-native-maps-specific perf pattern. 2) Halo communicates 'live' status without crowding the dot. 3) Selection state visually obvious.

title: Candle chart with pinch-zoom and per-candle tap details
design_brief: A crypto/stock candle chart: green up-candles, red down-candles, pinch to zoom timeline density, tap a candle to highlight + show OHLC values above the chart. Reference: Robinhood crypto candles, Coinbase advanced, Trading View mobile.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: SVG-based candle drawing (slow with hundreds of candles)
Re-rendering all candles on zoom
Hardcoded candle width
Tap detection via pixel-perfect bbox in JS
mobile_reasoning: Skia for the candles. Pinch sets a 'visibleCount' shared value; candles drawn from a window into the data. Tap converts x to candle index. Highlighted candle has thicker outline.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: CandleChart component with all-Skia drawing.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const CANDLE = {minWidth: 4, maxWidth: 16, gap: 1, wickWidth: 1, up: '#30D158', down: '#FF453A'};
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Visible count derived from canvas width / candleWidth.

animation_strategy: visibleCount via withSpring on pinch end.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pinch for zoom. Gesture.Tap for selection.
interaction_details: Pinch in: more candles visible (denser)
Pinch out: fewer candles, wider
Tap a candle: selection haptic + OHLC values shown above
Selected candle highlighted by outline (extend with derived value if needed)
accessibility_considerations: Chart container accessibilityRole='adjustable' for zoom via increment/decrement. Selected candle's OHLC announced via accessibilityLiveRegion.
performance_notes: Skia draws hundreds of candles in two paths (up/down) — minimal overdraw. Visibility window math in worklets.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Pinch-to-zoom timeline is a touch-native interaction. 2) Skia handles candle count where SVG would stutter. 3) Haptic on tap-select.

title: System share sheet integration for sharing text/links/files
design_brief: A 'Share' button that invokes the OS share sheet. Same content, different system UI on each platform. Reference: iOS UIActivityViewController, Android Intent.ACTION_SEND.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Custom share UI that doesn't include system options
Sharing only text when file/URL is more appropriate
Forgetting to handle 'share cancelled' result
mobile_reasoning: expo-sharing or react-native Share API gives access to the system share sheet. iOS shows UIActivityViewController; Android shows the system intent picker. URL vs file vs text auto-detected.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: useShare hook.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: Component-level sizing, spacing, and hit-target constants are named instead of hidden in inline styles.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: This task IS the difference — same call, two system UIs.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Layout uses available width, safe-area padding, and minimum 44px targets so phone and tablet density stay usable.

animation_strategy: Motion is bounded, cancellable, and tied to state changes the user can understand.
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Selection haptic on invoke
System sheet appears (UIActivityViewController on iOS, Intent picker on Android)
User picks app or cancels — both handled
File sharing routes through expo-sharing
accessibility_considerations: System share sheets are fully accessible by default.
performance_notes: Hot-path work is kept outside render loops; state updates happen only on committed user-visible changes.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Using the SYSTEM share sheet — every install app on the device shows up. 2) Platform-aware fallback for file sharing. 3) Cancellation handled silently.

title: Retro gaming UI: pixel font, scanline overlay, CRT corner glow
design_brief: A leaderboard or stat screen styled like an arcade cabinet: pixel font, neon green text on black, faint scanline overlay, slight CRT corner darkening. Reference: Game Boy Camera, Are.na pixel layouts, retro gaming apps.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: System font instead of pixel font
Sharp clean lines (CRT had scanlines, glow, slight blur)
Generic dark theme passed off as retro
mobile_reasoning: Custom pixel font via expo-font. Scanlines via Skia repeating thin lines at low opacity. Corner glow via radial gradient mask.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: RetroScreen wraps content with overlay layers.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Pixel font fixed at multiples of 8.

animation_strategy: Subtle scanline drift via withRepeat. Optional flicker.
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Slow scanline drift gives the screen subtle motion
Corner darkening adds CRT bezel feel
Text glow via textShadow
Pixel font everywhere
accessibility_considerations: Pixel font legibility is poor at small sizes — keep minimum 12pt. allowFontScaling true. Aria labels intact.
performance_notes: Scanline Skia path is cheap (just lines). Radial gradient is GPU. Drift updates pure shared value.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Skia overlay layered above standard RN content. 2) Custom font via expo-font. 3) textShadow gives glow with no extra views.

title: Search bar with cancel button slide-in (iOS pattern)
design_brief: The classic iOS search bar pattern: tappping the search input pushes the rest of the row aside to make room for a Cancel button that slides in from the right. Cancel dismisses the keyboard and clears focus. Reference: iOS Mail search, Settings search, Photos search.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Showing the Cancel button always (looks crowded when not focused)
No animation on Cancel reveal
Generic 'X' clear button (iOS has Cancel as text)
Using on Android (it's an iOS-specific convention)
mobile_reasoning: On iOS, the Cancel button appears only on focus and slides in from the right while the search bar shrinks to make room. Both transformations driven by a single 'focused' shared value via spring.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: IOSSearchBar component, Platform-gated.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: This is an iOS-only pattern; on Android use a different search affordance (Material standard).
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Bar width = parent width minus cancel width when focused.

animation_strategy: focused shared value 0/1. Cancel button translateX from cancelWidth to 0. Bar marginRight from 0 to cancelWidth.
gesture_strategy: Tap targets are at least 44px and form submission is explicit; no hidden gesture is required.
interaction_details: Focus: bar shrinks, Cancel slides in from right
Cancel tap: dismisses keyboard, clears text, blurs
Spring physics for both transforms
accessibility_considerations: Cancel button accessibilityLabel='Cancel search'. Input has accessibilityLabel='Search'.
performance_notes: Single shared value drives both animations. No re-renders.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Pixel-identical to native iOS UISearchBar. 2) Spring physics tuned to system curve. 3) Platform.OS gating means it doesn't ship on Android.

title: Modal stack with native presentation styles (sheet, fullscreenModal)
design_brief: Expo Router screens presented as iOS sheet (with grabber, swipe-down to dismiss), iOS fullScreenModal, and Android equivalent. Reference: iOS Mail compose, Cash App transaction detail, Calendar event create.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Custom modal containers when native presentation works
Forgetting Android adaptations
Sheet presentation that doesn't expose detents
mobile_reasoning: Expo Router exposes presentation prop on Stack.Screen — 'modal', 'transparentModal', 'fullScreenModal'. iOS 16+ supports sheet detents. Configure via screenOptions.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: app/_layout.tsx defines stack with multiple presentation modes.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: Component-level sizing, spacing, and hit-target constants are named instead of hidden in inline styles.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS uses UISheetPresentationController for sheet detents on iOS 15+; Android falls back to full screen.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Layout uses available width, safe-area padding, and minimum 44px targets so phone and tablet density stay usable.

animation_strategy: Native — handled by stack.
gesture_strategy: Swipe-down to dismiss on sheet — native.
interaction_details: compose: opens as sheet at medium detent, drag-up to expand, drag-down to dismiss
settings: standard modal
photo-viewer: full-screen with fade transition
Native swipe-down handled by iOS UISheetPresentationController
accessibility_considerations: Native presentation has full a11y support. accessibilityViewIsModal automatic.
performance_notes: Hot-path work is kept outside render loops; state updates happen only on committed user-visible changes.
what_makes_this_native: 1) iOS 16 sheet detents — the OS handles it. 2) Per-screen presentation config in Expo Router. 3) Native swipe-to-dismiss on sheets.

title: Reduce Motion compliance across all animations in an app
design_brief: A pattern for ensuring every animation in the app respects the system Reduce Motion accessibility setting. Animations either skip entirely or use simpler fades. Reference: Apple HIG, Material accessibility guidelines.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Animations that ignore the system setting
One-off if-checks at every call site
Reduce Motion that just makes animations SHORTER (still motion)
mobile_reasoning: Reanimated 3 provides useReducedMotion. Wrap animation primitives in a custom helper that returns no-op or simple fade when reduced motion is on.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: animation utilities file with safe wrappers.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: Component-level sizing, spacing, and hit-target constants are named instead of hidden in inline styles.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android expose reduce motion via the same RN AccessibilityInfo API.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Layout uses available width, safe-area padding, and minimum 44px targets so phone and tablet density stay usable.

animation_strategy: useReducedMotion gates all withSpring/withTiming calls.
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Reduce Motion ON: animations finish instantly or use 200ms fade only
Reduce Motion OFF: full spring/timing animations
No animation code change at call sites — just use motion helpers
accessibility_considerations: Every primary operation has a visible label, 44px target, and non-gesture path.
performance_notes: useReducedMotion subscribes once. No runtime cost per animation.
what_makes_this_native: 1) System accessibility setting respected automatically. 2) Layout animations have native Reduced Motion configs. 3) Centralized utility prevents drift.

title: Memoize-everything strategy for a scroll-heavy nested list
design_brief: A horizontal stories rail + vertical feed (Instagram pattern): stories scroll horizontally, feed scrolls vertically, both must stay smooth. Story tap should not re-render the feed. Reference: Instagram, Threads.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Inline arrow functions as renderItem (breaks memo)
Context that updates on every story tap (cascades re-renders)
ScrollView for stories (use FlashList horizontal)
mobile_reasoning: Memoize renderItem at module/component scope. Use stable refs for callbacks via useCallback. Per-story state via per-item subscribe pattern.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: HomeScreen with StoriesRail (horizontal FlashList) + Feed (vertical FlashList).
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Story size constant; feed item from rn-003.

animation_strategy: Motion is bounded, cancellable, and tied to state changes the user can understand.
gesture_strategy: Tap on story → open story viewer.
interaction_details: Horizontal scroll for stories, vertical for feed — no conflict (different orientations)
Tap story: selection haptic
Memo on items prevents cascade re-renders
accessibility_considerations: Each story has accessibilityRole='button' with user name + unseen status.
performance_notes: memo with custom equality. Stable callbacks via useCallback. FlashList recycling for both lists.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Per-item subscription via stable callbacks. 2) Nested scrolls coexist via orientation difference. 3) FlashList for both axes.

title: Calm/Japanese minimal: generous whitespace, thin type, single accent
design_brief: A meditation/journal app aesthetic inspired by MUJI, Calm app, and Studio Ghibli ephemera. Generous whitespace, thin light-weight type, one warm color accent, soft natural shadows. Reference: MUJI app, Calm, Headspace homescreen.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Dense layouts
Bold/heavy type
Multiple accent colors
Sharp corners (slight 4-6px radius)
mobile_reasoning: Restraint is the design. Type at 300 weight (Inter Thin or system equivalent). 24-48pt margins. Single accent color #E89B6F (warm orange). Soft shadows with high blur.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: theme.calm.ts. Components default to thin weight.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const CALM = {accent: '#E89B6F', text: '#2B2823', muted: '#8E867B', bg: '#FAF7F2', card: '#FFFFFF', font: 'Inter-Thin', fontMedium: 'Inter-Light'};
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Generous margins (24-32) regardless of screen.

animation_strategy: Very gentle: 400-600ms ease-in-out, no springs.
gesture_strategy: None custom.
interaction_details: Press cards with no scale (calm means restrained)
Soft shadows on cards
Wide margins frame each element
accessibility_considerations: Thin font weight at small sizes can fail contrast — keep body text minimum 16pt. Test against WCAG AA.
performance_notes: Hot-path work is kept outside render loops; state updates happen only on committed user-visible changes.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Thin custom font on mobile (rare). 2) Restraint defies mobile's compression-default. 3) Single accent communicates singularity of focus.

title: Like-button heart that bursts and lingers
design_brief: A heart button (Instagram-style) that on tap: scales up briefly, fills with red, and emits a few floating heart particles that drift up and fade. Reference: Instagram heart, Twitter like, Threads heart.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Just color change (boring)
Particles via Animated.Views (slow)
Particles that don't clean up
mobile_reasoning: Heart scale + color via Reanimated. Particles via Skia for performance. State machine: idle → bursting → settled.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: LikeButton component.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Fixed size.

animation_strategy: Scale: withSequence(spring 1.3, spring 1). Color: interpolateColor. Particles: per-particle position + opacity over 700ms.
gesture_strategy: Primary action is exposed through a Pressable with an accessibility action, with gestures treated as acceleration only.
interaction_details: Tap: light haptic + heart bursts (scale 1.3 then back)
Color crossfades white → red
5 small hearts drift up and fade
Re-tap: scales back, color crossfades back, no particles
accessibility_considerations: accessibilityRole='button' accessibilityState={{selected: liked}} accessibilityLabel={liked ? 'Unlike' : 'Like'}.
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Haptic on tap. 2) Worklet animations. 3) Particle effect adds delight without bloat.

title: Edge-pull-to-trigger action (Apollo-style swipe back)
design_brief: A Reddit-Apollo-style edge swipe: pull from left or right edge of screen to trigger an action (e.g., upvote/downvote on a comment, or navigate back). A floating icon appears that scales with pull distance, locking when threshold crossed. Reference: Apollo for Reddit comment swipes, Telegram swipe-reply.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Action triggers without visual progress indicator
No haptic at threshold
Pull too lenient (false triggers)
Pull doesn't cancel cleanly if dragged back
mobile_reasoning: Edge Pan gesture with hitSlop on screen edge. Icon scale + position interpolated from pull distance. Haptic fires once when crossing threshold. Release past threshold = action fires.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: EdgePullWrapper component with leftAction/rightAction props.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS and Android keep the same behavior; platform-specific shadow/elevation or picker APIs are isolated at the edge.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Fixed thresholds.

animation_strategy: Pull distance shared value. Icon style derived. Snap back with spring on release.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pan with hitSlop on left or right edge.
interaction_details: Pull from edge: icon fades in and scales
At 60px threshold: selection haptic
Release past threshold: action fires with success haptic
Release short of threshold: spring back, no action
accessibility_considerations: accessibilityActions on the wrapped content match the edge pull actions for VoiceOver users.
performance_notes: Edge gesture only active on hitSlop region — no conflict with content gestures.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Edge-pull is a touch-native gesture. 2) Haptic at threshold gives confidence without looking. 3) Icon scaling provides progress feedback.
